evidence_quotes.quote: In the videogame industry publishers have been selling games as products while their terms of service say they have the right to revoke the consumer's access to the product at any time, for any reason.
This would mean that publishers of videogames would be required to leave the game in a playable state when support for it ends.
These bundles are usually not matched to the cost of the available items in order to incentivize spending more money to get a little extra (larger bundles are usually discounted).

In the videogame industry publishers have been selling games as products while their terms of service say they have the right to revoke the consumer's access to the product at any time, for any reason.
This goes against the concept of purchasing goods, since the consumer does not get to own the product they paid for.

paragraphs.text: I strongly support this effort to strengthen consumer rights and I want it to be even stronger! In the videogame industry publishers have been selling games as products while their terms of service say they have the right to revoke the consumer's access to the product at any time, for any reason. Similar behavior is present in streaming of movies and tv. This goes against the concept of purchasing goods, since the consumer does not get to own the product they paid for. I want this Act to include protections in line with the Stop Killing Games movement, which is resposible for the Stop Destroying Videogames European Citizens's Initiative. This would mean that publishers of videogames would be required to leave the game in a playable state when support for it ends. I would also like to comment on the topic of virtual currency. I have never seen a game or an app that allows the user to purchase virtual currency in any way other than bundles of that currency. These bundles are usually not matched to the cost of the available items in order to incentivize spending more money to get a little extra (larger bundles are usually discounted). It also means that further purchases are justified with the idea that the user only needs a little bit more currency to buy another item, since they still have some currency over from the last purchase. This is obviously predatory and has negative effects on consumers.
